Octopus

An octopus is a soft-bodied, eight-limbed mollusc of the order Octopoda. Around 300 species are recognised, and the order is grouped within the class Cephalopoda with squids, cuttlefish, and nautiloids.

Like other cephalopods, an octopus is bilaterally symmetric with two eyes and a beaked mouth at the centre point of the eight limbs. The soft body can radically alter its shape, enabling octopuses to squeeze through small gaps.

Anatomy

Most octopuses have no internal or external skeleton, allowing them to fit through tight places. The only hard part of their body is the beak.

Behavior

Octopuses inhabit various regions of the ocean, including coral reefs, pelagic waters, and the seabed. They are highly intelligent invertebrates and have been observed using tools.
